team_spec.vision: A tool that tells a team whether their offers, needs, and commitments can route into a small shared pool, or what's blocking it.
team_spec.spec: Build a single-file Python tool, run as `python3 tool.py <pool_json_path>`, that reads the JSON file at the path given as its first command-line argument. The file is an object shaped { "pool_id": "...", "offers": [ { "id": "...", "contributor": "...", "kind": "...", "units": <number>, "consent_to_route": <boolean> }, ... ], "needs": [ { "id": "...", "requester": "...", "kind": "...", "units": <number> }, ... ] }. Print to stdout a routing diagnostic, in this order: (1) a header line exactly 'POOL ROUTING DIAGNOSTIC — <pool_id>'; (2) one blank line; (3) for each distinct kind (alphabetical), a block of: 'kind: <kind>' line; then 'offered: <O> units (<C> contributors consenting)' where O is the sum of units across offers of that kind whose consent_to_route is true and N is the count of contributors with at least one consenting offer; then 'needed: <D> units (<R> requesters)' where D is the sum of need units and R is the count of distinct requesters; then 'status: <status>' where status is 'fits' if O >= D and D > 0, 'short by <D-O> units' if O < D and D > 0, 'no demand' if D == 0 and O > 0, or 'no supply' if O == 0 and D > 0, or 'idle' if O == 0 and D == 0; then one blank line after each block (including the last); (4) a final 'BLOCKERS:' block: if any offers had consent_to_route == false, list each as '- <contributor> withheld consent on <kind> (<units> units)' alphabetical by contributor then kind; if no withholds, print 'BLOCKERS: none'. Normalize kind strings before grouping (strip + collapse internal whitespace + lowercase). Drop offers with non-numeric units or empty-normalized kind. Drop needs with non-numeric units or empty-normalized kind. If the input cannot be read or is not valid JSON, print a single line beginning with 'error:' and exit 0. Standard library only; no network; no credential access.
team_spec.build_target: one single-file command-line tool, standard library only, routing diagnostic output

allowed_inputs.name: sample-pool.json
allowed_inputs.note: small fictional pool with 5 offers across 3 kinds and 4 needs

excluded_inputs.boundary: Offers whose contributors withheld consent_to_route are recorded as blockers — the offer is named (contributor + kind + units) but is not routed and is not counted toward the offered total.
excluded_inputs.note: content withheld at the routing layer — surface as a blocker

excluded_inputs.boundary: Contributor contact details (email, phone, full name beyond display) are held by the team and never enter the build attempt or the diagnostic.
excluded_inputs.note: content withheld by the exporter — this record is named only

description: prints 'POOL ROUTING DIAGNOSTIC — <pool_id>' as the header
groups offers and needs by normalized kind (strip + collapse whitespace + lowercase)
for each kind: offered (only consenting), needed, and status
status: 'fits', 'short by N units', 'no demand', 'no supply', or 'idle'
BLOCKERS block lists non-consenting offers (contributor, kind, units) alphabetically
drops offers/needs with non-numeric units or empty-normalized kind
error: prefix on unreadable/invalid JSON, exit 0
stdlib only, no network, no credentials

review_checks: the generated tool runs and exits cleanly with no network, credential, or database access
the tool passes every acceptance-criteria smoke test
no excluded / marker-only record appears in the tool or its output
no outputs are hardcoded for specific inputs — the tool is correct in general
the witness record states only what happened and does not certify, approve, or grant reuse
the diagnostic does not 'route' anything — it only reports whether routing fits; no side effects beyond stdout
the diagnostic does not classify a withheld offer as a routing-failure, only as a blocker (consent, not capability)

fields: what did not work / what broke
witness_record_seed.receipt_statement: This record states what happened. It does not establish authority, approval, certification, legitimacy, community consent, or readiness for reuse.
